description: Maps every shipping asset from the runtime manifest to Ultima VIII style references and historical context.

player.historicalNotes: Portuguese adventurer or mixed-heritage merchant's agent in 1580 Melaka. Would wear a doublet, hose, and soft boots typical of middling Portuguese colonials, adapted for tropical heat with lighter fabrics.

fernao-gomes.historicalNotes: Wealthy Portuguese spice merchant. Would wear a fine doublet with slashed sleeves, a short cape, and a merchant's cap. Prosperous build reflecting success in the lucrative pepper and clove trade.

capitao-rodrigues.historicalNotes: Portuguese fortress guard captain at A Famosa. Would wear a morion helmet, breastplate over padded gambeson, and carry a partisan or sword. Military bearing and upright posture define the silhouette.

padre-tomas.historicalNotes: Jesuit priest at St. Paul's Church. Would wear a black Jesuit cassock with a white collar, a wide-brimmed capello romano hat. Tall, thin silhouette distinguishable by the long unbroken robe line.

aminah.historicalNotes: Malay market vendor. Would wear a baju kurung (long tunic and sarong) in batik-patterned fabric with a tudung (headscarf). Silhouette reads as distinctly Malay through the sarong drape and head covering.

chen-wei.historicalNotes: Chinese guild representative at the waterfront. Would wear a Ming-dynasty changshan (long robe) with a round collar and a guanmao cap. Upright bearing with hands often clasped, reflecting merchant-scholar status in the Chinese trading community.

rashid.historicalNotes: Arab sailor and navigator from a docked dhow. Would wear a thobe or loose shirt with a turban or keffiyeh. Weathered, lean build of a maritime trader on the monsoon route between Arabia and the Malay Archipelago.

siti.historicalNotes: Malay woman, likely a local resident of the kampung quarter. Would wear a traditional baju kurung with a kain samping wrap. Hair tied or covered with a selendang shawl, reflecting Malay cultural modesty norms of the period.

alvares.historicalNotes: Portuguese colonial figure. Would wear standard Portuguese attire of the era: doublet, trunk hose, and a felt hat. Likely an official or merchant tied to the Estado da India administration in Melaka.

mak-enang.historicalNotes: Malay elder woman, a respected community figure in the kampung. Would wear a loose baju and batik sarong with a shoulder cloth. Older, stouter build signaling matriarchal authority in Malay village life.

gaspar-mesquita.historicalNotes: Portuguese noble or high-ranking official in Melaka. Would wear an elaborate doublet with puffed sleeves, a short cloak, and a plumed hat, reflecting the fidalgo class that governed Portuguese Malacca under the captain-general.

diogo-almeida.historicalNotes: Portuguese figure, possibly a soldier or lower official. Standard colonial doublet and hose, perhaps with a leather jerkin. Part of the garrison or administrative apparatus of the Estado da India's Melaka outpost.

lin-mei.historicalNotes: Chinese woman in the Melaka trading community. Would wear a Ming-era ruqun (cross-collar blouse and skirt) or ao (jacket) with her hair in a traditional bun. Part of the established Chinese merchant families who had traded in Melaka since the Sultanate era.

pak-salleh.historicalNotes: Malay man, an elder or community leader in the kampung. Would wear a baju melayu (tunic) with a sarong and a songkok cap. Carries himself with the quiet authority of a local headman navigating life under Portuguese colonial rule.

portuguese.historicalNotes: Generic Portuguese colonist. Doublet and hose silhouette, European hat.

portuguese-guard.historicalNotes: Portuguese soldier silhouette. Morion helmet and weapon define the shape at 8x16.

portuguese-worker.historicalNotes: Portuguese laborer. Simpler tunic, possibly carrying a load. Dockworker or construction hand.

portuguese-priest.historicalNotes: Catholic priest or friar. Long robe silhouette with tonsured head or wide hat.

malay.historicalNotes: Malay man. Baju melayu tunic and sarong silhouette with songkok cap.

malay-woman.historicalNotes: Malay woman. Baju kurung and headscarf silhouette, often carrying a basket.

malay-child.historicalNotes: Malay child. Smaller proportions, simple tunic. Adds life and scale reference to crowd scenes.

chinese.historicalNotes: Chinese merchant or laborer. Long changshan robe or shorter work tunic with cap.

arab.historicalNotes: Arab trader or sailor. Thobe and turban or keffiyeh silhouette.

indian.historicalNotes: Indian trader from Gujarat or Malabar coast. Dhoti and turban silhouette, part of the Tamil and Gujarati merchant communities in Melaka.

fortress-stone.historicalNotes: Laterite stone used in A Famosa fortress construction, quarried locally.

wall-white.historicalNotes: Whitewashed plaster walls typical of Portuguese colonial architecture in tropical climates.

roof-terracotta.historicalNotes: Portuguese-style terracotta roof tiles, a hallmark of Estado da India architecture.

cobblestone.historicalNotes: Cobblestone paving on Rua Direita, the main commercial street of Portuguese Melaka.

door-wood.historicalNotes: Heavy wooden doors typical of Portuguese colonial and Malay merchant buildings.

church-stone.historicalNotes: Dressed stone for St. Paul's Church, built atop Bukit Melaka overlooking the strait.

church-floor.historicalNotes: Polished stone floor inside St. Paul's Church, cooler and smoother than exterior paving.

dock-wood.historicalNotes: Timber dock planking at the waterfront quay where Portuguese carracks and local prahu unloaded.

water-tile.historicalNotes: Melaka Strait water surface. Warm tropical waters where the monsoon trading routes converge.

dirt-path.historicalNotes: Packed earth paths in the kampung quarter and outskirts of the Portuguese settlement.

bamboo-floor.historicalNotes: Split bamboo flooring in Malay kampung houses, raised on stilts above the ground.

thatch-roof.historicalNotes: Attap palm-leaf thatch roofing on Malay kampung dwellings.

grass-tropical.historicalNotes: Lush tropical grass and undergrowth around Melaka's less-developed areas.

sand-beach.historicalNotes: Sandy shore along the Melaka coastline where fishing boats are beached.

fortress-stone.historicalNotes: Isometric laterite fortress stone for A Famosa.

cobblestone.historicalNotes: Isometric cobblestone paving for Rua Direita.

bamboo-floor.historicalNotes: Isometric bamboo floor tile for kampung houses.

church-stone.historicalNotes: Isometric dressed stone for St. Paul's Church.

altar.historicalNotes: Catholic altar in St. Paul's Church, with crucifix and candles.

amphora.historicalNotes: Mediterranean-style storage vessel brought by Portuguese traders.

anchor.historicalNotes: Iron ship anchor at the waterfront, from Portuguese carracks or local vessels.

arched-window.historicalNotes: Portuguese colonial arched window, common in Melaka's European quarter.

awning.historicalNotes: Cloth market awning providing shade on Rua Direita.

balance-scale.historicalNotes: Merchant's balance scale for weighing spices and trade goods.

bamboo-fence.historicalNotes: Bamboo fence in the kampung quarter, common boundary marker.

banana-tree.historicalNotes: Banana plant, ubiquitous in tropical Melaka and a staple food source.

barrel.historicalNotes: European-style wooden barrel for wine, water, or dry goods storage.

bell.historicalNotes: Church bell or ship's bell, cast bronze.

bench.historicalNotes: Simple wooden bench, found in public areas and church grounds.

betel-nut-tray.historicalNotes: Betel nut chewing set, a widespread social custom across Southeast Asian cultures.

bush.historicalNotes: Tropical shrub, common landscaping around colonial and local buildings.

candelabra.historicalNotes: Multi-branched candle holder in the church or wealthy merchant homes.

cannon.historicalNotes: Portuguese bronze cannon defending A Famosa fortress, key to holding the Strait.

cargo-crate.historicalNotes: Large shipping crate for spices, textiles, or porcelain at the waterfront.

ceramic-vase.historicalNotes: Chinese or local ceramic ware, a major trade commodity in Melaka.

coconut.historicalNotes: Coconut, essential food and material in Malay daily life.

coconut-water-stand.historicalNotes: Street vendor stand selling coconut water, a common refreshment.

cooking-fire.historicalNotes: Open cooking fire in the kampung, used for preparing rice and fish dishes.

dhow-sail.historicalNotes: Lateen sail from an Arab dhow, the workhorse of Indian Ocean trade.

drying-fish-rack.historicalNotes: Wooden rack for drying fish in the sun, a Malay preservation method.

fish-basket.historicalNotes: Woven rattan basket for carrying fresh catch from the strait.

fish-trap.historicalNotes: Bubu fish trap woven from bamboo, traditional Malay fishing gear.

fishing-net.historicalNotes: Cast net draped over the dock or hung to dry after fishing.

flowers.historicalNotes: Tropical flowers: frangipani, hibiscus, or bougainvillea.

fruit-basket.historicalNotes: Basket of tropical fruit: durian, rambutan, mangosteen, sold at market.

gravestone.historicalNotes: Portuguese tombstone at St. Paul's churchyard, many dating to early colonial period.

handcart.historicalNotes: Wooden handcart for moving goods between the waterfront and market.

hanging-cloth.historicalNotes: Dyed cloth or batik hanging to dry or displayed for sale at market.

hanging-oil-lantern.historicalNotes: Oil lantern hung from buildings or poles for nighttime illumination.

herb-drying-rack.historicalNotes: Rack for drying herbs and spices, part of Melaka's spice processing trade.

iron-fence.historicalNotes: Wrought iron fence around Portuguese colonial buildings or the church compound.

lantern.historicalNotes: Portable oil or candle lantern, Chinese or Portuguese design.

laundry-line.historicalNotes: Clothes drying on a line, a domestic detail adding life to residential areas.

maritime-barrel.historicalNotes: Pitch-sealed barrel for ship stores: water, salt pork, or gunpowder.

market-stall.historicalNotes: Open market stall on Rua Direita, the commercial heart of Portuguese Melaka.

market-stall-1.historicalNotes: Market stall variant with awning, selling spices or textiles.

market-stall-2.historicalNotes: Market stall variant, possibly a food vendor or craftsman's booth.

moored-sampan.historicalNotes: Small Malay flat-bottomed boat moored at the waterfront or riverbank.

palm-tree.historicalNotes: Coconut palm, defining feature of the Melaka coastal landscape.

pelourinho.historicalNotes: Portuguese pillory column, symbol of colonial justice and authority in the town square.

pottery.historicalNotes: Local or imported earthenware pots used for cooking and storage.

prayer-mat.historicalNotes: Muslim prayer mat, reflecting the Islamic faith of the Malay and Arab communities.

rice-mortar.historicalNotes: Wooden mortar and pestle for husking rice, staple food preparation tool.

rice-pot.historicalNotes: Clay or iron pot for cooking rice over an open fire.

rope-coil.historicalNotes: Coiled hemp or coconut-fiber rope at the docks.

sack.historicalNotes: Burlap or cloth sack for rice, spices, or other dry goods.

scroll-rack.historicalNotes: Rack for documents or maps in a counting house or church sacristy.

ship-mast.historicalNotes: Tall ship mast visible at the waterfront, from a Portuguese carrack or nau.

spice-pile.historicalNotes: Heaped spices at market: cloves, nutmeg, mace, or cinnamon.

spice-pile-pepper.historicalNotes: Black pepper pile, Melaka's most valuable trade commodity by volume.

stone-cross.historicalNotes: Stone crucifix or padrao marker, symbol of Portuguese Catholic presence.

sugar-cone.historicalNotes: Conical sugar loaf, a valuable European trade item in the spice exchange.

tavern-sign.historicalNotes: Hanging wooden sign for a tavern on Rua Direita, serving wine and arrack.

tin-ingots.historicalNotes: Tin ingots from Malay Peninsula mines, a major export commodity.

water-well.historicalNotes: Stone or brick well providing fresh water in the settlement.

wayang-kulit-puppet.historicalNotes: Leather shadow puppet from Malay wayang kulit tradition, an ancient storytelling art.

wine-jug.historicalNotes: Ceramic wine jug, imported from Portugal or produced locally.

wooden-pew.historicalNotes: Church pew in St. Paul's, carved from local tropical hardwood.

woven-mat.historicalNotes: Woven pandanus leaf mat, used for sitting and sleeping in Malay households.

torch-flame.historicalNotes: Torch flame animation for wall-mounted or standing torches.

palm-frond.historicalNotes: Swaying palm frond animation, driven by tropical breeze.

awning-flutter.historicalNotes: Market awning cloth fluttering in the wind.

smoke-column.historicalNotes: Rising smoke from cooking fires or forges.

seagull.historicalNotes: Seagull wing-flap animation, waterfront atmosphere element.

gradeCard.notes: Shadow direction scoring not applicable to flying bird animation. Shadow check overridden — seagull is an atmospheric element, not a ground-anchored object.

flag-wave.historicalNotes: Portuguese flag waving at A Famosa fortress, bearing the royal coat of arms.

player.historicalNotes: Portuguese or mixed-heritage adventurer. VGA portrait should show European features with sun-weathered skin, reflecting life in tropical Melaka.

fernao-gomes.historicalNotes: Wealthy Portuguese spice merchant. Portrait should convey prosperity: well-fed features, trimmed beard, rich fabric doublet with merchant's cap.

capitao-rodrigues.historicalNotes: Portuguese fortress guard captain. Stern military bearing, morion helmet or bare-headed with close-cropped hair, weathered face of a colonial soldier.

padre-tomas.historicalNotes: Jesuit priest. Tonsured head or skullcap, black cassock with white collar, scholarly and ascetic features reflecting the intellectual Jesuit order.

aminah.historicalNotes: Malay market vendor. Warm, open expression of a trader. Wearing tudung headscarf, with visible batik fabric at the neckline. Southeast Asian features.

chen-wei.historicalNotes: Chinese guild representative. Composed, calculating expression. Ming-era guanmao cap, round-collar changshan visible. East Asian features reflecting the Hokkien merchant diaspora.

rashid.historicalNotes: Arab sailor and navigator. Weathered, jovial expression. Turban or keffiyeh, sun-darkened skin from years on Indian Ocean monsoon routes. Arab features.

siti.historicalNotes: Malay woman. Gentle features framed by a selendang shawl. Southeast Asian complexion and features typical of the Malay Peninsula.

alvares.historicalNotes: Portuguese official or merchant. European features, possibly with a goatee, wearing a felt cap and doublet appropriate to his station.

mak-enang.historicalNotes: Malay elder woman. Lined face showing age and wisdom, wearing a plain headcloth. Respected matriarchal figure in kampung society.

gaspar-mesquita.historicalNotes: Portuguese fidalgo. Aristocratic bearing, groomed beard, plumed hat or bare head showing high forehead. Rich doublet with slashed sleeves visible.

diogo-almeida.historicalNotes: Portuguese soldier or minor official. Rough-hewn features, stubble, practical attire. A working colonial rather than an aristocrat.

lin-mei.historicalNotes: Chinese woman from the Melaka trading community. Hair in a traditional bun with a decorative pin, Ming-era cross-collar visible. East Asian features of the Hokkien diaspora.

pak-salleh.historicalNotes: Malay elder man. Dignified bearing with songkok cap, greying facial hair. The face of local Malay authority in the kampung under Portuguese rule.

opening-screen.historicalNotes: Title screen vista of 1580 Melaka, showing the fortress, strait, and layered cityscape.

scene-a-famosa.historicalNotes: A Famosa fortress gate, the iconic laterite stone entrance to Portuguese Melaka, built by Afonso de Albuquerque after 1511.

scene-a-famosa-dawn.historicalNotes: A Famosa at dawn, warm golden light from the east over the Strait of Melaka.

scene-a-famosa-dusk.historicalNotes: A Famosa at dusk, orange and purple sky as the sun sets behind the peninsula.

scene-a-famosa-night.historicalNotes: A Famosa at night, torchlit fortress walls under a tropical starfield.

scene-rua-direita.historicalNotes: Rua Direita, the main commercial street of Portuguese Melaka, lined with shops, taverns, and market stalls.

scene-rua-direita-dawn.historicalNotes: Rua Direita at dawn as merchants open stalls and early traders arrive.

scene-rua-direita-dusk.historicalNotes: Rua Direita at dusk, golden light on whitewashed walls as the market winds down.

scene-rua-direita-night.historicalNotes: Rua Direita at night, oil lanterns and tavern light spilling onto cobblestones.

scene-st-pauls.historicalNotes: St. Paul's Church atop Bukit Melaka, a stone chapel built by the Portuguese with panoramic views of the strait.

scene-st-pauls-dawn.historicalNotes: St. Paul's Church at dawn, sunrise over the Strait illuminating the stone facade.

scene-st-pauls-dusk.historicalNotes: St. Paul's Church at dusk, silhouetted against the sunset sky.

scene-st-pauls-night.historicalNotes: St. Paul's Church at night, candlelit interior glimpsed through windows.

scene-waterfront.historicalNotes: The Waterfront Quay, where Portuguese carracks, Malay prahu, Arab dhows, and Chinese junks crowd the harbor.

scene-waterfront-dawn.historicalNotes: Waterfront at dawn, fishing boats returning with the night's catch.

scene-waterfront-dusk.historicalNotes: Waterfront at dusk, ships' masts silhouetted against the darkening sky.

scene-waterfront-night.historicalNotes: Waterfront at night, lanterns on anchored ships reflecting on dark water.

scene-kampung.historicalNotes: The Kampung Quarter, a Malay village of stilted wooden houses with attap roofs amid coconut palms and tropical vegetation.

scene-kampung-dusk.historicalNotes: Kampung at dusk, warm light through bamboo walls as families gather.

scene-kampung-night.historicalNotes: Kampung at night, firefly light and distant oil lamps in the tropical darkness.
